title: Building a cold compost heap from pallets
subtitle: Three-pallet bay for slow composting of garden and kitchen waste
excerpt: A three-pallet bay takes two hours to build, costs nothing if you can get free pallets, and processes up to 600 litres of organic waste per fill. Cold composting takes 6-18 months to produce usable compost, but requires no turning and no thermometer. This is the low-effort starting point for most compost setups.
sourceNotes: RHS composting guidance (rhs.org.uk). WRAP compost-at-home guidance. Garden Organic recommended compost heap dimensions.

content.text: is the default for most garden composters. Material goes in as it is generated, the heap breaks down slowly, and finished compost is extracted from the base after 6-18 months. A pallet bay provides structure, airflow through the slats, and enough volume for the process to work.
Three standard pallets (1,200 × 800 mm is the most common size). UK pallets are widely available free from builders merchants, garden centres, and DIY stores.
Wire or screws to fix the corners.
A piece of old carpet or cardboard to cover the top (retains moisture).

Site the heap on bare soil, not on hard-standing. Contact with soil allows worms to migrate into the heap from below and drainage to work naturally. A partially shaded spot slows drying out in summer.
Step 2, stand the back pallet upright.

Stand one pallet as the back wall. Stand the two side pallets perpendicular, touching the back corners. Wire or screw the corners, two screws at each joint is sufficient. The front is left open for loading and turning.

in roughly equal layers. Start with a layer of coarse browns (wood chip, torn cardboard) at the base for drainage. Add kitchen scraps in the middle of the heap rather than at the surface to discourage flies.

Place old carpet or cardboard over the top to retain moisture. The heap should be damp but not waterlogged, it should feel like a wrung-out sponge. Check monthly and add water if the surface feels dry.

items.fix: Add a thick layer of torn cardboard or wood chip. Turn the heap if possible to aerate it.
items.cause: Too many greens and not enough browns, or the heap is waterlogged and anaerobic

items.fix: Add water until the heap feels damp. Add a layer of grass clippings or fresh food waste to add nitrogen.
items.cause: Too dry or too few greens to drive decomposition
items.symptom: Nothing seems to be decomposing after months

items.fix: Never add cooked food, meat, fish, or dairy to an open heap. Bury fresh food scraps under a thick layer of browns.
items.cause: Cooked food or meat added to an open heap
